serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of spaghetti al nero di seppia is approximately 380 g, comprising a portion of cooked spaghetti dressed with squid ink sauce, calamari rings, tiger prawns, and cherry tomatoes.

nutrient_source.title: Pasta with seafood sauce, cooked — composite estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central entries: Spaghetti cooked (FDC ID 20124), Squid mixed species cooked (FDC ID 15149), Shrimp mixed species cooked (FDC ID 15152), and Tomatoes cherry raw (FDC ID 11529)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA or HPB entry exists for spaghetti al nero di seppia as a composite dish. Values are estimated from a weighted blend of USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries: cooked spaghetti (~55% by weight), cooked squid/calamari (~15%), cooked tiger prawns (~15%), cherry tomatoes (~8%), olive oil (~5%), and squid ink (~2%). Squid ink contributes negligible macronutrients but significant iron and antioxidants. Weighted average computed per 100 g of finished dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Spaghetti, white, boiled (GI ~46)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No GI value exists specifically for spaghetti al nero di seppia. GI of 46 is taken from white spaghetti boiled (al dente), as reported in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care) and the University of Sydney GI Database. Squid ink, calamari, prawns, and tomatoes contribute negligible glycemic carbohydrates and do not materially alter the GI of the pasta base. GL per serving calculated as: (46 × 18.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 380 g serving) / 100 = approximately 32.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg per 100 g; ~1178 mg per restaurant serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that a full restaurant serving of this dish may contribute approximately 50% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; portion control and requesting reduced-salt preparation are advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~32.5 per serving) from refined pasta carbohydrates
type2_diabetes.guidance: Although the glycemic index of spaghetti is low (GI ~46), the large carbohydrate load per restaurant serving (~70 g carbohydrate) produces a moderate-to-high glycemic load; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size, pair with high-fibre vegetables, and monitor postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat content with beneficial omega-3 fatty acids from seafood
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The seafood components (calamari and tiger prawns) provide lean protein and omega-3 fatty acids while saturated fat remains low (~0.7 g per 100 g), making this dish a relatively heart-friendly pasta option when prepared with olive oil and consumed in moderate portions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Spaghetti al Nero di Seppia is a classic Italian pasta dish originating from the coastal regions of Sicily and Southern Italy, now widely served in Italian restaurants globally including Singapore. The dish features long spaghetti strands coated in a rich, briny squid ink sauce that imparts a distinctive jet-black colour and a subtle oceanic umami flavour. It is typically garnished with tender calamari rings, succulent tiger prawns, and sweet cherry tomatoes, all sautéed in olive oil with garlic and often finished with white wine. The texture combines the al dente bite of the pasta with the soft chew of the seafood. Nutritionally, the dish provides a moderate energy density (~148 kcal per 100 g), meaningful protein from the seafood (~9.2 g per 100 g), low saturated fat, and a notable vitamin B12 content from the molluscs and crustaceans. The glycemic index is low (~46) due to the pasta structure, though the glycemic load per full restaurant serving is moderate (~32.5) owing to the large carbohydrate portion. Sodium content is elevated in restaurant preparations and warrants attention for individuals managing hypertension. The squid ink itself is rich in antioxidants and iron. This dish is suitable for pescatarians and is dairy-free, but contains gluten, shellfish, and mollusc allergens.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for spaghetti al nero di seppia as a composite restaurant dish. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted blend of USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for the component ingredients. GI is taken from the closest analogous food (white spaghetti boiled) from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level USDA data is high quality and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-weighted estimates per 100 g of finished dish based on USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data; no single composite entry exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or USDA for this specific dish. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entries for the component ingredients at a level that can be reliably aggregated. 3. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g; this is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy data for the component ingredients (olive oil, seafood, pasta) — no industrial trans fats are present. 4. GI of 46 is an analogous estimate from white spaghetti boiled (al dente) per University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008; squid ink and seafood components do not materially alter the glycemic response of the pasta base. 5. Sodium estimate of 310 mg per 100 g reflects a typical restaurant preparation with added salt and squid ink sauce; actual sodium may vary significantly by restaurant. 6. The dish is not halal-certified at the listed restaurant (Ricciotti - The Riverwalk) as it may contain wine in the sauce preparation. 7. Monounsaturated fat 2.1 g and polyunsaturated fat 0.6 g are estimated from olive oil and seafood lipid profiles per USDA data. 8. Iron content (~1.8 mg per 100 g) is notably elevated due to squid ink contribution. 9. Vitamin B12 (~1.4 mcg per 100 g) is a meaningful contribution from calamari and tiger prawns.

Iteration 2: Resolved aliases consistency issue by ensuring all aliases_by_language entries appeared verbatim in flat aliases list; removed canonical food_name from aliases.

Confirmed all 20 entries match exactly including Tamil script ('ஸ்பகெட்டி அல் நீரோ டி செப்பியா', 'மை இறால் பாஸ்தா'), Hindi Devanagari ('स्पेगेटी अल नीरो दी सेप्पिया', 'काली स्पेगेटी'), and all Chinese-script entries. No missing entries found. Q8 consistency rule fully satisfied.
